Buying puts without owning underlying Buying puts without owning underlying Buying puts without owning underlying Buying puts without owning underlying Buying puts without owning underlying **Rationale:** Buying put options without owning the underlying asset is a common options trading strategy used for speculation or hedging. A put option gives the holder the right, but not the obligation, to sell a specific amount of an underlying asset at a predetermined price (the strike price) before the option expires. When an investor buys puts without owning the underlying stock, they are essentially betting that the price of the stock will decline. If the stock price drops below the strike price, the value of the put increases, allowing the investor to profit by selling the option at a higher premium or exercising it to sell stock at a higher strike price

Do high interest rates lead to higher bond yields or lower? Do high interest rates lead to higher bond yields or lower? High interest rates generally lead to higher bond yields.   **Rationale:**   Bond yields and interest rates are inversely related to bond prices. When interest rates rise, existing bonds with lower fixed interest payments become less attractive compared to new bonds issued at the higher prevailing rates. As a result, the prices of existing bonds decrease to offer a comparable yield to new bonds. This decrease in bond prices corresponds to an increase in their yields. Conversely, when interest rates fall, existing bonds with higher fixed payments become more attractive, driving their prices up and their yields down.  **Answer:**   High interest rates lead to higher bond yields.
